TextENGNew: Several days had passed.
changeReason: Old says 'A few days had passed since the incident'; new says 'Several days had passed' — the old adds 'since the incident' which is not in the Japanese (それから数日がたった simply means 'several days had passed since then').

TextENGNew: Nothing in particular had changed. I went to school on weekdays, and after class we all battled it out in club activities.
Win or lose, we made a tremendous ruckus and had ourselves a good time, then headed home.
After that, dinner, a bath, deep sleep. Then morning again.
On weekends we'd head into Okinomiya or go hang out at someone's house, losing track of time and carrying on until just before supper.
There weren't any particularly dramatic changes or incidents, but everything felt fresh and exciting—filled with both fun and a quiet contentment.
Ahh, so this is what it means to have happy, fulfilling times—thoughts like that drifted through my mind now and then.
In between all that, Mion and the others asked me to help set up the venue for the village festival—the Watanagashi—which was coming up soon.
Helping out with the preparations alongside the adults was hard physical work, but the sense of accomplishment more than made up for it.
Most of all, I was genuinely happy to feel as though I'd been accepted as a true member of the village.
And before I knew it, the day of the Watanagashi had finally arrived.
Ding-dong~~!
"Keiichi-kun, are you home~~!!"
"Oh my, it's Rena-chan! Thank you so much for always looking after my Keiichi...!"
"Ah... O-Okaa-san... I-It's I who... has been looked after by..."
changeReason: Old translates おばさま as 'Ma'am'; new translates as 'Okaa-san' — the Japanese おばさま (auntie/madam) is a polite address for a friend's mother, and translating it differently changes the social context. 'Okaa-san' retains the Japanese honorific.

TextENGNew: "Ha~i, Kei-chan! You come hungry today? We're filling up at the food stalls tonight!"
"You think I'd waste stomach space knowing the club's going to the food stalls?!"
"Now that's the spirit!"
"Where are Satoko and Rika-chan? Already at the shrine?"
changeReason: Old says 'Where are Rika-chan and Satoko?' listing Rika first; new says 'Where are Satoko and Rika-chan?' listing Satoko first — the Japanese lists 沙都子 (Satoko) first, matching the new translation's order. Also 'temple' vs 'shrine' (神社 = shrine, not temple).

TextENGNew: "Yep! Rika-chan is on the festival committee, after all. Satoko-chan is probably with her."
"I see. ...All right! Let's make some noise tonight!!"
"Hey~!! You're all late, everyone!!"
"It is simply unacceptable to keep a lady waiting, Keiichi-san~!!!"
"Oh? My apologies. And where might this 'lady' I've kept waiting be?"
"Wha-wha-wha-WHAAAAT did you just saAaay!?!?"
"...Oh my~☆ ...Rika-chan... S-s-s-s-so... cu~te...♪!!"
"...Good evening, everyone. Good evening to you as well, Keiichi."
"Kyaa!! Looking good, looking good! You're wearing it again this year!"
changeReason: Old says 'You're looking sharp again this year!' (complimenting appearance); new says 'You're wearing it again this year!' (commenting on wearing something) — different focus: looking good vs wearing an outfit, per Japanese キマってる which means 'looking good/stylish.'

TextENGNew: "S-so cute, so cute...! Gonna take you hoome~!!"
With that, the usual club members had assembled, and the fun evening of the Watanagashi festival began.
June 20, 1983 — Before Dawn
changeReason: Old translates 未明 as 'early morning'; new translates as 'Before Dawn' — 未明 specifically means 'before dawn/predawn,' which is more precise than 'early morning.'

TextENGNew: A few hours after the Hinamizawa village festival had ended——@r@rDeep in the mountains, somewhere along the prefectural border between Gifu and Aichi
changeReason: Old treats 山中 as a place name ('In Yamanaka'); new correctly translates it as 'deep in the mountains.' Also 'somewhere on the border' vs 'somewhere along the border' is minor.
